enrichment.short_description: Crunchyroll is a subscription video streaming service that specializes in anime, manga, and related Japanese entertainment, offering simulcasts of new episodes alongside a large back catalog of licensed titles. It is owned and operated by Sony through Crunchyroll, LLC, following the 2021 consolidation of Crunchyroll, Funimation, Wakanim, and VRV into a single brand. The platform combines video streaming, a manga reading library, and a curated games catalog under tiered subscription plans.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium tiered subscription: an ad-supported free tier plus three paid monthly or annual plans
enrichment.starting_price: 9.99 USD per month for the Fan plan, or 66.99 USD per year on the discounted Fan Annual plan
enrichment.seo_title: Crunchyroll Review 2026: Pricing, Plans and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Crunchyroll costs in 2026, how its Fan, Mega Fan and Ultimate Fan plans differ, and whether the anime streaming service is worth it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crunchyroll is the largest legal anime streaming service outside Japan, founded in 2006 in San Francisco by UC Berkeley computer science graduates Kun Gao, James Lin, Vu Nguyen, and Brandon Ooi. It began as a video-sharing site for fan-subtitled anime before pivoting into a licensed streaming platform that simulcasts new episodes within hours of their Japanese broadcast. Sony Pictures Television acquired a controlling stake in the anime distributor Funimation in 2017 and later purchased Crunchyroll itself from AT and T and WarnerMedia in 2020 for 1.175 billion dollars, completing the deal in 2021. In March 2022, Sony merged Crunchyroll, Funimation, Wakanim, and the VRV service into a single unified brand operated as Crunchyroll, LLC, headquartered in San Francisco with additional major offices in Coppell, Texas (the former Funimation headquarters) and Culver City, California. As of 2026 the company reports roughly 21 million paid subscriptions and around 2,000 employees worldwide. The service is priced on three paid tiers in the United States: Fan at 9.99 dollars per month with ad-free viewing and single-device offline downloads; Mega Fan at 13.99 dollars per month adding four simultaneous streams, HD offline downloads, and access to the Crunchyroll Game Vault; and Ultimate Fan at 17.99 dollars per month adding six simultaneous streams and full access to Crunchyroll Manga. A discounted Fan Annual plan is available for 66.99 dollars per year. These prices reflect a 2026 increase from the prior 7.99 and 11.99 dollar tiers. Crunchyroll's core catalog spans thousands of anime series and films across genres such as shonen, isekai, slice of life, and sports, plus a growing slate of Crunchyroll-branded original productions and co-productions with Japanese studios. The platform supports apps on smart TVs, game consoles, mobile devices, and web browsers, with offline downloads, multi-device profiles, and parental controls. Competitively, Crunchyroll differentiates itself from general-purpose streamers like Netflix, Max, and Disney Plus by focusing entirely on anime and by simulcasting new episodes on the same day they air in Japan, which is a major draw for fans who want to avoid spoilers. It also differentiates itself from anime-adjacent services by bundling a manga reading library and a games catalog into its top subscription tier, something none of its direct competitors, such as HIDIVE, currently replicate at the same scale. Common criticisms include recurring price increases since 2023, advertising on the free ad-supported tier, occasional licensing splits where certain high-profile titles stream exclusively on rival platforms like Netflix or Max, and simultaneous-stream limits that require upgrading to unlock multi-device household use. For buyers researching Crunchyroll in 2026, the key facts are: it is Sony-owned, headquartered in San Francisco, founded in 2006, priced from 9.99 dollars per month, and built around simulcast anime, manga, and games in a single subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: History and ownership
paragraphs: Crunchyroll was founded in 2006 by four UC Berkeley graduates as a site for streaming anime, later transitioning to a fully licensed model as it signed distribution deals with Japanese studios. Sony Pictures Television built its anime holdings through two major acquisitions: a controlling stake in Funimation in 2017, followed by the purchase of Crunchyroll from AT and T and WarnerMedia in a deal completed in 2021 for 1.175 billion dollars.

In 2022, Sony merged Crunchyroll, Funimation, Wakanim, and VRV into one unified brand under the entity Crunchyroll, LLC, ending years of overlapping catalogs and separate apps. The company is now the primary anime distribution and streaming arm of Sony's Aniplex and Funimation Global Group operations, headquartered in San Francisco with major operational hubs in Coppell, Texas and Culver City, California.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Crunchyroll offers
paragraphs: Beyond video streaming, Crunchyroll's higher tiers bundle a manga reading library and a games catalog, positioning the service as a broader anime entertainment hub rather than a single-purpose video app. It simulcasts new episodes of ongoing anime seasons, often within an hour of the Japanese broadcast, and maintains a large library of dubbed and subtitled classics and current-season titles.
The platform is available on the major streaming devices, smart TVs, consoles, and mobile operating systems, with offline downloads and profile-based multi-device access gated by subscription tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Crunchyroll pricing works
paragraphs: Crunchyroll uses a three-tier paid structure in the US market, with each step up unlocking more simultaneous streams, higher-quality offline downloads, and additional content categories such as manga and games. An annual Fan plan offers a lower effective monthly rate for subscribers willing to commit upfront.
Pricing has increased over the past two years as Sony has invested more heavily in original productions and licensing, a pattern common across the wider streaming industry.

features.title: Day-of-broadcast simulcasts
features.description: New episodes of ongoing anime seasons are typically available within an hour of their original Japanese television broadcast.

features.title: Large licensed anime catalog
features.description: Thousands of subtitled and dubbed series and films spanning decades, from classic titles to current-season releases.

features.title: Crunchyroll Manga
features.description: Included with the Ultimate Fan plan, giving access to hundreds of digital manga titles alongside the video catalog.

features.title: Offline downloads
features.description: Episodes can be downloaded for offline viewing, with resolution and device limits that scale by subscription tier.

features.title: Multi-device streaming
features.description: Higher tiers allow up to four or six simultaneous streams on different devices under one account.

features.title: Crunchyroll Game Vault
features.description: A curated library of mobile and casual games included with Mega Fan and Ultimate Fan subscriptions.

features.title: Ad-free viewing
features.description: All three paid tiers remove advertising from the video player, unlike the limited free tier.

features.title: Broad device support
features.description: Native apps for smart TVs, game consoles, mobile devices, and web browsers.

pros: One of the largest legally licensed anime catalogs available in Western markets
Simulcasts new episodes close to their original Japanese air date
Top-tier plan bundles manga and games alongside video for one price
Offline downloads make it practical for commuting or travel
Wide device and platform support across TVs, consoles, and mobile
cons: Subscription prices have risen twice in recent years across all tiers
The free tier is limited and includes advertising
Some high-profile titles are licensed exclusively to rival platforms
Simultaneous stream limits require upgrading for larger households
Regional catalog availability can vary by country due to licensing

faqs.question: What is Crunchyroll
faqs.answer: Crunchyroll is a subscription streaming service focused on anime, manga, and related Japanese entertainment, owned by Sony and operated as Crunchyroll, LLC.

faqs.question: How much does Crunchyroll cost in 2026
faqs.answer: In the United States, Fan is 9.99 dollars per month, Mega Fan is 13.99 dollars per month, and Ultimate Fan is 17.99 dollars per month, with an annual Fan discount available.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Crunchyroll
faqs.answer: Yes, Crunchyroll offers a limited ad-supported free tier, though it lacks the full catalog access and features of the paid plans.

faqs.question: Does Crunchyroll have dubbed anime
faqs.answer: Yes, a large portion of the catalog includes English dubs alongside subtitled Japanese audio, though not every title has a dub available.

faqs.question: Can I download anime for offline viewing on Crunchyroll
faqs.answer: Yes, offline downloads are available on all paid tiers, with the number of devices and download quality increasing on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Who owns Crunchyroll
faqs.answer: Crunchyroll is owned by Sony, operating under Crunchyroll, LLC after the 2022 merger of Crunchyroll, Funimation, Wakanim, and VRV.

faqs.question: Does Crunchyroll include manga
faqs.answer: Manga access is included specifically with the Ultimate Fan plan through Crunchyroll Manga.

faqs.question: How many simultaneous streams does Crunchyroll allow
faqs.answer: Fan allows one device, Mega Fan allows four simultaneous streams, and Ultimate Fan allows six.

use_cases.title: Weekly simulcast viewing
use_cases.description: Fans who want to watch new anime episodes as close as possible to the Japanese broadcast without waiting for a licensed dub.
use_cases.audience: Active anime fans following ongoing seasonal series

use_cases.title: Household multi-device streaming
use_cases.description: Families or roommates who need several people to stream different titles at the same time on one subscription.

use_cases.title: Offline commuting and travel
use_cases.description: Subscribers who download episodes in advance to watch anime without an internet connection during commutes or flights.

use_cases.title: Combined video and manga reading
use_cases.description: Readers who want to follow a manga series alongside its anime adaptation using a single Ultimate Fan subscription.
use_cases.audience: Manga readers and anime crossover fans

enrichment.short_description: Crypteron was a developer-focused data encryption and key management platform that added application-layer security, including automated encryption, tamper protection, and audit trails, to backend applications built in .NET or Java. Founded in San Diego, California, it targeted enterprises and regulated industries needing HIPAA, PCI, and GDPR-style compliance. As of 2026, the company has officially concluded operations and its website displays a shutdown notice rather than an active signup flow.
enrichment.pricing_model: Formerly freemium, with a free Community edition and paid per-app subscription tiers; the service is discontinued and no longer accepting new customers
enrichment.starting_price: Not applicable, Crypteron has ceased operations
enrichment.company_size: Small (reported in the range of a few dozen employees or fewer in its later years)
enrichment.headquarters: La Jolla, San Diego, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Crypteron: What It Was and Why the Service Shut Down
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crypteron offered application-layer data encryption for developers. Here is what it did, its pricing history, and why the service is no longer active.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crypteron was a data security and encryption-as-a-service platform aimed at software developers and enterprises, founded in 2013 and headquartered in La Jolla, San Diego, California, by Wolfgang Wedemeyer, Sid Shetye, and Yaron Guez. Its core product added application-layer encryption to backend systems, meaning developers could encrypt sensitive fields, files, and database columns without redesigning their data architecture. The platform used AES-GCM encryption with off-site, automatically rotated key management, tamper detection to flag unauthorized data modifications, and a one-click breach response feature that could suspend key management operations during a suspected security incident. Integration was built around SDKs and attribute- or annotation-based code markup for .NET and Java applications, which let engineering teams add encryption to specific fields with minimal code changes rather than adopting a full encryption framework. A related product, CipherDB, focused specifically on database-level encryption. Crypteron marketed itself around compliance use cases, citing support for HIPAA, PCI, and GDPR-oriented requirements, and it offered a free Community edition for development use alongside paid production tiers priced per application, with custom Enterprise pricing that included FIPS 140-2 certification, phone and email support, and a much higher number of security partitions and access control rules. As of 2026, however, Crypteron's own website states, quote, after more than 13 years of serving our customers, Crypteron has officially concluded operations, end quote, thanking its customers and partners. The site no longer supports new account registrations, and legacy pages such as documentation, release notes, and the pricing page remain online largely as historical artifacts rather than an active commercial offering. No further public detail is available on the exact shutdown date, the reason for closure, or what migration path, if any, was offered to remaining customers, so any of those specifics should be treated as undisclosed rather than assumed. For a directory or research context, the most accurate framing of Crypteron in 2026 is as a discontinued, historically notable data-encryption tool: it is useful to reference for developers researching what application-layer encryption services looked like in the 2013 to roughly mid-2020s era, or for teams that adopted Crypteron in the past and now need to identify a replacement, but it should not be recommended as an active option for new projects. Companies searching for a currently operating equivalent would need to look at alternative application-layer encryption, key management, or tokenization vendors, since Crypteron itself is no longer onboarding customers, charging subscriptions, or actively developing its SDKs. Any pricing figures associated with Crypteron, including its historical free Community tier and its custom Enterprise quotes, should be understood as legacy pricing that is no longer purchasable rather than current, live pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Crypteron was
paragraphs: Crypteron was a security-as-a-service platform that let developers add encryption, key management, and tamper protection to backend applications at the application layer, rather than relying solely on database- or disk-level encryption. It targeted .NET and Java developers with SDKs that used code attributes or annotations to mark sensitive fields for automatic encryption.

The product suite included a general encryption and key management platform plus CipherDB, a dedicated database encryption offering, along with a management dashboard for access control policies and audit trails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current status: discontinued
paragraphs: Crypteron's website now states that the company has officially concluded operations after more than 13 years of serving customers, and new account registrations are closed. Legacy marketing and documentation pages remain publicly accessible, but there is no indication the product is still being actively sold, supported, or developed.
No public source specifies the exact date of closure or the underlying reason, so those details should be treated as not publicly disclosed rather than inferred.

long_form_blocks.heading: Compliance focus and typical use cases
paragraphs: While active, Crypteron positioned itself around regulated industries, citing support for HIPAA, PCI, and GDPR-oriented compliance requirements, and marketed to enterprise CTOs and CISOs as well as smaller software companies that needed to demonstrate strong data protection practices without building custom cryptography in-house.

features.title: Application-layer encryption
features.description: Encrypted specific fields, files, or database columns using AES-GCM without requiring a full data architecture redesign.

features.title: Off-site key management and rotation
features.description: Managed encryption keys remotely with automated rotation to reduce the operational burden of key lifecycle management.

features.title: Tamper protection
features.description: Detected unauthorized modifications to protected data and flagged them for review.

features.title: One-click breach response
features.description: Allowed administrators to suspend key management operations quickly during a suspected security incident.

features.title: CipherDB database encryption
features.description: A dedicated product for encrypting data at the database layer.

features.title: .NET and Java SDKs
features.description: Attribute- and annotation-based integration that let developers mark sensitive fields for encryption with minimal code changes.

features.title: Compliance-oriented feature set
features.description: Built-in support intended to help teams meet HIPAA, PCI, and GDPR-style requirements.

features.title: Access control and audit trail dashboard
features.description: A management console for defining security partitions, access rules, and reviewing audit logs.

pros: Simple SDK-based integration for .NET and Java applications
Compliance-oriented feature set aimed at regulated industries
Automated key rotation reduced manual key management overhead
Breach response tooling allowed fast lockdown during incidents
Cloud-agnostic design worked across public, private, and hybrid deployments while it was active
cons: The service has officially shut down and cannot be adopted for new projects
No public detail on the exact shutdown date or reason for closure
Existing customers must migrate to an alternative encryption provider
Platform support was historically limited to .NET and Java, excluding other stacks
Documentation and release notes are no longer actively maintained

faqs.question: Is Crypteron still available
faqs.answer: No. Crypteron's website states it has officially concluded operations after more than 13 years, and new registrations are closed.

faqs.question: What did Crypteron do
faqs.answer: It provided application-layer data encryption, key management, tamper protection, and audit trails for backend applications, primarily for .NET and Java developers.

faqs.question: When did Crypteron shut down
faqs.answer: The exact shutdown date is not publicly disclosed; the company's site only states it concluded operations after more than 13 years in business.

faqs.question: Was Crypteron free to use
faqs.answer: It offered a free Community edition for development use, with paid production and custom Enterprise tiers, though none of these are purchasable now that the service has closed.

faqs.question: What can I use instead of Crypteron
faqs.answer: Teams that previously relied on Crypteron need to evaluate other application-layer encryption, key management, or tokenization vendors, since Crypteron is no longer onboarding customers.

faqs.question: Who founded Crypteron
faqs.answer: Crypteron was founded in 2013 by Wolfgang Wedemeyer, Sid Shetye, and Yaron Guez, based in San Diego, California.

faqs.question: Does Crypteron still offer support
faqs.answer: There is no indication of active customer support since the company states it has concluded operations.

faqs.question: Is Crypteron's documentation still online
faqs.answer: Legacy documentation and release notes pages remain publicly accessible, but they describe a product that is no longer actively sold or maintained.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for application-layer encryption
use_cases.description: Researchers and developers studying how early encryption-as-a-service platforms approached compliance-driven data protection.

use_cases.title: Migration planning for former customers
use_cases.description: Organizations that previously integrated Crypteron and now need to select a replacement encryption or key management vendor.

use_cases.title: Compliance case study

enrichment.short_description: Crystallize is a headless commerce and product information management (PIM) platform built around a single GraphQL API that combines catalog data, editorial content, and native subscription commerce. Founded in Norway in 2017 by brothers Bard Farstad and Aleksander Farstad, it targets engineering-led teams building custom storefronts with complex product modeling needs. Pricing is usage-based, with a free tier and metered plans scaling up to enterprise contracts.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based subscription with a free tier plus metered API calls, bandwidth, orders, and items; higher tiers add flat monthly fees
enrichment.starting_price: Free on the Particle plan, or 949 USD per month plus metering on the Atom plan
enrichment.seo_title: Crystallize Review 2026: Headless Commerce and PIM Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crystallize combines PIM, CMS, and commerce behind one GraphQL API. See its pricing tiers, features, pros and cons for headless storefronts.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crystallize is a Norwegian headless commerce and product information management platform built entirely around a GraphQL API, founded in 2017 by brothers Bard Farstad (CEO) and Aleksander Farstad (Chairman) as a venture incubated by their accelerator New Normal Group. The company is headquartered in Skien, Telemark, Norway, and reports between 11 and 50 employees. Crystallize's core positioning is that it merges functionality normally split across separate PIM, CMS, commerce, and subscription-billing systems into one GraphQL data layer, with shape-based content modeling that lets teams define custom product and content structures rather than being locked into a rigid schema. Because the platform was designed API-first and GraphQL-native from the start, its marketing emphasizes that the data is inherently structured and queryable in a way that suits both custom storefront development and newer AI agent-driven commerce integrations. Key product capabilities include native subscription commerce (recurring billing and subscription offerings built into the core commerce engine rather than bolted on), a combined catalog and content model so marketing content and product data live in the same API, multi-tenant project support, and an AI-assisted content operations feature called Flare that consumes AI tokens as part of usage-based billing. Pricing follows a three-tier, usage-metered structure. The Particle plan is free with metered overage charges beyond generous included limits (25 orders, 100 items, 5 gigabytes of bandwidth, and 25,000 API calls per month), making it viable for hobbyists, prototypes, and small tests. The Atom plan costs 949 USD per month plus metering, raising the included limits to 500 orders, 5,000 items, 50 gigabytes of bandwidth, and 500,000 API calls, and adds a professional service level agreement. The top-tier Crystal plan is custom-priced, includes three tenants, enterprise features such as SAML and SSO, tenant cloning, a dedicated Slack support channel, an enterprise SLA, and access to professional services and advisory support, with the lowest per-unit overage rates of the three tiers. All tiers support unlimited users and unlimited languages, reflecting the platform's focus on multi-market, multilingual commerce. Because Crystallize is headless, it does not ship a themed storefront out of the box; instead, businesses build a custom frontend, commonly with Next.js-based boilerplates the company provides, which gives design flexibility at the cost of requiring more engineering investment than a templated platform like Shopify. This makes Crystallize best suited to teams with in-house engineering resources tackling complex catalog structures, B2B pricing rules, or subscription-based revenue models, rather than small merchants wanting a quick, no-code storefront. Competitors in the same space include other headless commerce and composable commerce platforms such as commercetools, Shopify's headless offerings, and Contentful-plus-commerce-layer combinations, with Crystallize differentiating itself through its unified single-API approach and native subscription support. As of 2026, Crystallize continues to operate as an independent, Norway-based company with transparent, publicly listed usage-based pricing rather than the fully custom, sales-gated pricing common among some enterprise headless commerce vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company background
paragraphs: Crystallize was founded in 2017 in Norway by brothers Bard Farstad and Aleksander Farstad, incubated through their venture accelerator New Normal Group before becoming an independent company. It is headquartered in Skien, Telemark, and employs between 11 and 50 people.

The founders describe the company's mission around applying craftsmanship principles to both the product and the way the company operates, aiming to build what they call the ultimate headless commerce service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Platform architecture
paragraphs: Crystallize unifies product information management, content management, commerce, and subscription billing behind a single GraphQL API, using shape-based modeling so teams can define custom structures for products and content rather than working within a fixed schema.
Because the platform is GraphQL-native, it is positioned to suit both traditional custom storefront development and newer use cases where AI agents query structured commerce data directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Crystallize pricing works
paragraphs: Crystallize prices on a three-tier, usage-metered model spanning a free Particle tier, a 949 USD per month Atom tier, and a custom-priced Crystal enterprise tier, with metering based on orders, catalog items, bandwidth, and API calls.
All tiers include unlimited users and unlimited languages, and overage rates drop significantly on higher tiers, meaning the effective per-unit cost falls as usage scales.

features.title: Unified GraphQL API
features.description: Combines PIM, CMS, and commerce data in a single API rather than requiring separate systems stitched together.

features.title: Shape-based content modeling
features.description: Lets teams define custom product and content structures instead of using a fixed schema.

features.title: Native subscription commerce
features.description: Recurring billing and subscription offerings are built into the core commerce engine.

features.title: Multi-tenant support
features.description: Higher tiers include multiple tenants per project for managing separate storefronts or markets.

features.title: AI-assisted content operations (Flare)
features.description: AI-assisted tooling for content operations, billed based on tokens consumed.

features.title: Unlimited languages and users on every plan
features.description: All pricing tiers support unlimited team members and unlimited storefront languages.

features.title: Next.js storefront boilerplates
features.description: Starter frontend templates that speed up building a custom headless storefront.

features.title: Enterprise SSO and tenant cloning
features.description: SAML and single sign-on support along with tenant cloning tools on the top-tier Crystal plan.

pros: Single GraphQL API reduces the need to integrate separate PIM, CMS, and commerce systems
Transparent, publicly listed usage-based pricing including a genuinely free tier
Native subscription commerce support without third-party billing add-ons
Flexible shape-based modeling suits complex or unconventional catalog structures
Overage rates decrease significantly at higher usage tiers
cons: Requires custom frontend development since there is no themed storefront out of the box
Smaller ecosystem and community than larger commerce platforms like Shopify
Usage-based metering can make costs less predictable at scale
Enterprise Crystal tier pricing is fully custom and not listed publicly
Learning curve for teams unfamiliar with GraphQL-first architectures

faqs.question: What is Crystallize
faqs.answer: Crystallize is a headless commerce and PIM platform that combines catalog data, content, and subscription commerce behind a single GraphQL API.

faqs.question: How much does Crystallize cost
faqs.answer: Crystallize offers a free Particle plan with metered overages, an Atom plan at 949 USD per month plus metering, and a custom-priced Crystal enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Who founded Crystallize
faqs.answer: Crystallize was founded in 2017 by brothers Bard Farstad and Aleksander Farstad in Norway.

faqs.question: Does Crystallize include a storefront template
faqs.answer: No, Crystallize is headless and requires building a custom frontend, though it provides Next.js boilerplates to speed up development.

faqs.question: Does Crystallize support subscription billing
faqs.answer: Yes, native subscription commerce is built into the core platform rather than requiring a separate billing integration.

faqs.question: Where is Crystallize headquartered
faqs.answer: Crystallize is headquartered in Skien, Telemark, Norway.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan
faqs.answer: Yes, the Particle plan is free with generous included usage limits and metered overage pricing beyond that.

faqs.question: Who is Crystallize best suited for
faqs.answer: Engineering-led teams building custom headless storefronts with complex product modeling, subscriptions, or B2B pricing needs.

use_cases.title: Subscription-based product catalogs

use_cases.title: Complex B2B catalogs
use_cases.description: Companies with intricate product variants, pricing rules, or multi-market catalogs that outgrow templated ecommerce platforms.

use_cases.title: Content-heavy commerce sites
use_cases.description: Brands that need editorial content and product data to live in the same API for fast, unified storefront queries.
use_cases.audience: Marketing and engineering teams building custom storefronts

use_cases.title: Multilingual, multi-tenant storefronts
use_cases.description: Organizations running several localized storefronts or brands from a shared underlying catalog.

enrichment.short_description: Cscope is a free, open-source, terminal-based tool for browsing and navigating large C source code bases, letting developers quickly find function definitions, callers, symbol usages, and text patterns across a codebase. It originated at Bell Labs in the early 1980s, was contributed to open source under a BSD license in 2000, and remains in active use today, notably as a supported navigation target in the Linux kernel build system.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source under the BSD license
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, a volunteer-maintained open-source project rather than a company
enrichment.headquarters: Originally developed at Bell Labs in Murray Hill, New Jersey, United States; now maintained as a distributed open-source project without a fixed headquarters
enrichment.seo_title: Cscope: Free C Source Code Browser and Navigation Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cscope is a free, open-source terminal tool for navigating large C codebases. Learn its history, features, and how it compares to modern IDEs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cscope is a text-based source code browsing and navigation tool created by Joe Steffen in the early 1980s while working at Bell Labs, originally to help him navigate his own C programming work on a PDP-11 system. It grew in popularity internally at AT and T and Bell Labs as colleagues requested additional features, and it was eventually distributed across every AT and T location worldwide through the company's internal exptools network for experimental tools, becoming a dependency for many large internal projects with codebases reaching into the millions of lines. The tool later became part of the official AT and T Unix distribution, passing through several corporate owners as the Unix business changed hands, from Unix System Laboratories to Novell and then to The Santa Cruz Operation (SCO). In April 2000, SCO announced it was contributing cscope to the open-source community under a BSD license, and SCO engineer Petr Sorfa became its first open-source maintainer, notably porting the codebase to build with the GNU gcc toolchain. Maintenance responsibilities later passed to Hans-Bernhard Broker and Neil Horman, and the project has continued to be maintained on SourceForge and, more recently, in community forks on GitHub, with the last widely referenced stable release being version 15.9 from July 2018. Functionally, cscope builds a searchable database from a codebase and then lets developers run fast lookups for where a function or symbol is defined, where it is called, where a text string or regular expression appears, and where a particular file includes another file, all from a lightweight terminal interface. It integrates tightly with classic Unix editors, most notably vim and Emacs, letting developers jump directly from a search result to the corresponding line in their editor. While its native support targets C source code, it can also process C++, Java, lex, and yacc files, though with some limitations compared to full multi-language IDEs. Because of its speed and low resource footprint, cscope remains a preferred navigation tool for developers working on very large, low-level C codebases, most famously the Linux kernel, which ships a dedicated make target specifically for building a cscope database of the kernel source tree. It is also cited as valuable for security auditing and reverse engineering work, where quickly tracing how a function or variable is used across an unfamiliar, sprawling codebase is essential. Compared to modern IDE-based navigation in tools like Visual Studio Code, CLion, or language-server-based editors, cscope's terminal interface is dated and it lacks built-in refactoring or semantic type-aware navigation, and its index must be manually rebuilt to stay accurate after code changes. However, its minimal dependencies, fast performance even on huge codebases, and ability to work seamlessly over SSH on remote or embedded development machines keep it in active use among systems programmers, kernel developers, and security researchers who work primarily in terminal environments. As free, open-source software with no company behind it, cscope has no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or commercial support offering; support comes from community maintainers and long-standing documentation rather than a vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Origins at Bell Labs
paragraphs: Cscope was written by Joe Steffen in the early 1980s to help navigate his own C programming work on a PDP-11 at Bell Labs. It quickly spread across AT and T and Bell Labs as colleagues requested more features, becoming a dependency for large internal projects with codebases reaching into the millions of lines, and it was distributed globally through AT and T's internal exptools network.

The tool later became part of the official AT and T Unix distribution and passed through several corporate owners, including Unix System Laboratories, Novell, and The Santa Cruz Operation, before SCO contributed it to open source under a BSD license in April 2000.

long_form_blocks.heading: How cscope works
paragraphs: Cscope builds a searchable index database from a source tree and then provides fast lookups for function definitions, function callers, symbol references, text or regular expression matches, and file inclusion relationships, all through a lightweight terminal interface.

It integrates closely with vim and Emacs, letting developers jump from a search result directly to the matching line of code in their editor, and it processes C++, Java, lex, and yacc files in addition to its native C support, though with some limitations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Modern relevance and limitations
paragraphs: Cscope remains actively used for navigating very large, low-level C codebases, most notably the Linux kernel, which includes a dedicated build target for generating a cscope database, and it is frequently cited as a useful tool for security auditing of unfamiliar code.

Its terminal-based interface is dated compared to modern IDE navigation, and it lacks built-in refactoring or semantic, type-aware search, requiring the index to be manually rebuilt after code changes to stay accurate.

features.title: Function and symbol cross-referencing
features.description: Quickly locate where a function or variable is defined, called, or referenced across an entire codebase.

features.title: Text and regular expression search
features.description: Search for arbitrary text patterns or regular expressions across all indexed source files.

features.title: Fast indexed database
features.description: Builds a searchable database once so subsequent lookups are fast even on codebases with millions of lines.

features.title: Editor integration
features.description: Integrates with vim and Emacs to jump directly from search results to the matching line of code.

features.title: Multi-language support beyond C
features.description: Can process C++, Java, lex, and yacc files in addition to its native C focus.

features.title: Interactive and command-line modes
features.description: Supports both an interactive terminal browsing session and scriptable command-line queries.

features.title: Low resource footprint
features.description: Runs efficiently over SSH and on constrained or remote development machines.

features.title: Linux kernel build integration
features.description: The Linux kernel source tree includes a dedicated make target for generating a cscope database.

pros: Extremely lightweight and fast even on codebases with millions of lines
No external dependencies, making it easy to install on minimal or remote systems
Works well over SSH, unlike many GUI-based navigation tools
Long track record of use in security auditing and kernel development
Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
cons: Terminal-based interface feels dated compared to modern IDE navigation
Limited native language support focused mainly on the C family
No built-in refactoring tools, unlike modern language-server-based editors
Index must be manually rebuilt to stay accurate after code changes
Less discoverable and beginner-friendly than mainstream IDE search features

faqs.question: What is cscope used for
faqs.answer: Cscope is used to browse and navigate large C source code bases, finding function definitions, callers, symbol references, and text matches quickly.

faqs.question: Who created cscope
faqs.answer: Joe Steffen created cscope in the early 1980s while working at Bell Labs.

faqs.question: Is cscope free
faqs.answer: Yes, cscope is free and open source under a BSD license, contributed to open source by SCO in April 2000.

faqs.question: Does cscope work with languages other than C
faqs.answer: It primarily targets C but can also process C++, Java, lex, and yacc files, with some limitations.

faqs.question: Does cscope integrate with vim or Emacs
faqs.answer: Yes, cscope has well-established integrations with both vim and Emacs for jumping from search results directly into the editor.

faqs.question: Is cscope still maintained in 2026
faqs.answer: Yes, cscope continues to be maintained by community volunteers on SourceForge and in GitHub forks, though its most referenced stable release, version 15.9, dates to July 2018.

faqs.question: Why does the Linux kernel use cscope
faqs.answer: The Linux kernel source tree includes a dedicated build target for generating a cscope database because the tool handles navigation of the kernel's very large C codebase efficiently.

faqs.question: How is cscope different from an IDE
faqs.answer: Cscope is a lightweight, terminal-based indexing and search tool without the semantic type-awareness or refactoring features of a full IDE, but it is faster and has far fewer dependencies.

use_cases.title: Linux kernel and low-level systems development
use_cases.description: Navigating extremely large C codebases such as the Linux kernel using a dedicated cscope database build target.

use_cases.title: Security auditing of unfamiliar codebases
use_cases.description: Tracing how functions and variables are used across a large, unfamiliar C codebase during a security review.

use_cases.title: Remote and terminal-only development
use_cases.description: Navigating source code efficiently over SSH on remote servers or embedded development environments without a GUI.

enrichment.short_description: CudaText is a free, open-source, cross-platform text and code editor written in Object Pascal using the Lazarus IDE, created by developer Alexey Torgashin as a successor to his earlier SynWrite editor. First released around 2015, it emphasizes fast startup, low resource usage, and extensibility through a Python plugin system, with native builds available for Windows, Linux, macOS, and FreeBSD.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, usable for personal and commercial purposes at no cost
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, an independently developed open-source project rather than a company
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable, open-source project with its lead developer based in Russia
enrichment.seo_title: CudaText: Free Open-Source Code Editor Review
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CudaText is a free, open-source cross-platform code editor with Python plugins and 300+ syntax lexers. See its features, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CudaText is a free, open-source, cross-platform text and source code editor created by developer Alexey Torgashin, first released around 2015 as the successor to his earlier Windows editor SynWrite after that project's development was discontinued. The application's core is written in Object Pascal using the Lazarus IDE, which gives it a small footprint, fast startup time (roughly 0.3 seconds with around 30 plugins loaded, according to the project's own benchmarks), and native builds for Windows, Linux, macOS, and FreeBSD without relying on a heavier cross-platform framework like Electron. CudaText is licensed under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, making it free to use for both personal and commercial purposes, and its source code is publicly maintained on GitHub under the Alexey-T/CudaText repository, with an active release history of incremental version updates. The editor ships with an extensive built-in feature set aimed at developers and technical writers: syntax highlighting through more than 300 bundled lexers covering a very wide range of programming and markup languages, a code tree panel that shows the structure of functions, classes, and other symbols for languages whose lexer supports it, code folding, multiple carets and multiple selections for simultaneous multi-point editing, find and replace with regular expression support, and the ability to split the editing window into two, three, four, or six groups of tabs for working across several files at once. It includes a command palette with fuzzy matching for quickly executing commands by name, a minimap and micromap for visual navigation of large files, and a binary and hex viewer capable of handling files of effectively unlimited size, including multi-gigabyte log files that would overwhelm many conventional text editors. The single biggest differentiator for CudaText is its extensibility: it supports add-ons written in Python, covering plugins, linters, custom code tree parsers for additional languages, and external tool integrations, and it ships with several preinstalled plugins such as a Project Manager for organizing multi-file projects and a Snippets system that supports the same snippet format used by Visual Studio Code. Because CudaText has no company or commercial entity behind it, it has no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or paid support offering; all funding and development effort comes from its lead maintainer and open-source community, with support delivered through GitHub issues, the Free Pascal community wiki, and user forums rather than a formal customer support channel. Compared to modern mainstream editors such as Visual Studio Code or Sublime Text, CudaText's plugin ecosystem is considerably smaller and it lacks the deep language server protocol and IntelliSense-style integrations that dominate contemporary coding workflows, and its interface design reads as more utilitarian than polished. However, its low resource requirements, fast startup even with many plugins loaded, native support for less common platforms like FreeBSD, and genuinely free-for-commercial-use licensing make it a practical choice for developers on older or resource-constrained hardware, for portable USB-based development setups, and for anyone who wants a lightweight, script-extensible editor without subscription costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Origins and licensing
paragraphs: CudaText was created by developer Alexey Torgashin around 2015 as the successor to his earlier Windows-only editor SynWrite, after that project's development was discontinued. The editor's core is written in Object Pascal using the Lazarus IDE.
It is released under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, making it free for both personal and commercial use, with its source code publicly maintained on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core editing features
paragraphs: CudaText ships with more than 300 bundled syntax lexers, a code tree panel for viewing file structure, code folding, multiple carets and selections, regular-expression find and replace, and the ability to split the editor window into up to six groups of tabs.

It also includes a fuzzy-matching command palette, minimap and micromap navigation aids, and a binary and hex viewer capable of opening files of effectively unlimited size, including multi-gigabyte logs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Extensibility through Python plugins
paragraphs: The editor's defining feature is its Python-based add-on system, which covers plugins, linters, custom code tree parsers, and external tool integrations, giving it functional depth well beyond its lightweight core.
It ships with several preinstalled plugins, including a Project Manager for multi-file project organization and a Snippets system compatible with the Visual Studio Code snippet format.

cons: Smaller plugin ecosystem than mainstream editors like Visual Studio Code or Sublime Text
Lacks deep language server protocol and IntelliSense-style integrations found in modern editors
Interface design is more utilitarian than polished compared to newer competitors
Documentation is largely community-driven and less comprehensive than commercial alternatives
Some plugins require a separate Python installation to function

faqs.question: Is CudaText free
faqs.answer: Yes, CudaText is free and open source under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, usable for personal and commercial projects at no cost.

faqs.question: Who created CudaText
faqs.answer: CudaText was created by developer Alexey Torgashin as the successor to his earlier editor SynWrite.

faqs.question: What programming language is CudaText written in
faqs.answer: CudaText's core is written in Object Pascal using the Lazarus IDE.

faqs.question: Does CudaText support plugins
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports an extensive Python-based plugin system covering linters, code tree parsers, and external tool integrations.

faqs.question: What platforms does CudaText support
faqs.answer: CudaText has native builds for Windows, Linux, macOS, and FreeBSD.

faqs.question: How many languages does CudaText support for syntax highlighting
faqs.answer: CudaText ships with more than 300 built-in syntax lexers covering a wide range of programming and markup languages.

faqs.question: Can CudaText open very large files
faqs.answer: Yes, its built-in binary and hex viewer can handle files of effectively unlimited size, including multi-gigabyte logs.

faqs.question: Is CudaText a good replacement for SynWrite
faqs.answer: Yes, CudaText was created specifically as the successor to SynWrite after that editor's development was discontinued.

enrichment.short_description: Cura, commonly known as Ultimaker Cura or UltiMaker Cura, is a free, open-source 3D printing slicer that converts 3D model files into printer-ready instructions. Originally created by David Braam and later adopted and maintained by Ultimaker, it is now developed by UltiMaker following the 2022 merger of Ultimaker and MakerBot. The software offers more than 400 configurable print settings and is reported to serve over one million users worldwide.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source desktop application under the LGPLv3 license; UltiMaker Cura Enterprise is available separately for businesses via custom quote
enrichment.starting_price: Free for the standard desktop application; custom pricing for UltiMaker Cura Enterprise
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 400 employees at UltiMaker, the company that maintains Cura
enrichment.seo_title: Ultimaker Cura Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ultimaker Cura is a free open-source 3D printing slicer with 400-plus settings. See its features, licensing, pros, cons, and Enterprise pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cura, now branded UltiMaker Cura, is a free and open-source 3D printing slicer application that converts digital 3D model files into the layer-by-layer machine instructions, known as G-code, that fused filament fabrication 3D printers use to build physical objects. The software was originally created by David Braam, who was later hired by the Dutch 3D printer manufacturer Ultimaker to continue developing and maintaining it as the company's flagship slicing software. Ultimaker itself was founded in 2011 in the Netherlands by Martijn Elserman, Erik de Bruijn, and Siert Wijnia, and the company's headquarters later moved to Utrecht. In September 2022, Ultimaker completed a merger with rival 3D printer maker MakerBot, forming a combined company branded UltiMaker, backed by investors NPM Capital and Stratasys; the merged company continues to develop and maintain Cura as its primary slicing product alongside MakerBot's existing hardware lines such as the METHOD and SKETCH printers. UltiMaker now employs roughly 400 people and is headquartered in Utrecht, Netherlands. Cura's licensing has changed over its history: it originally launched under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3, then switched to the more permissive LGPLv3 license on September 28, 2017, specifically to allow easier integration with third-party CAD applications. The software is reported to be used by more than one million people globally, processing roughly 1.4 million print jobs every week, and it was named Software Tool of the Year at the 2019 3D Printing Industry Awards in London. Functionally, Cura offers more than 400 individually configurable print settings covering layer height, infill density and pattern, print speed, support structures including advanced tree supports, adaptive layers, and multi-material or multi-extruder printing, giving both beginners using simplified presets and advanced users fine-grained control the same underlying engine. It supports importing standard 3D model formats including STL, OBJ, X3D, and 3MF, ships with an extensive built-in library of printer profiles covering hundreds of desktop 3D printers beyond just UltiMaker and MakerBot hardware, and includes a layer-by-layer print preview for inspecting the sliced result before printing. A plugin marketplace lets users add community-built extensions, and Cura Connect and the UltiMaker Digital Factory cloud service extend the software with remote print monitoring and print queue management for multiple networked printers, primarily aimed at UltiMaker's own hardware. For organizations that need more stability and governance than the standard rolling-release desktop app, UltiMaker offers Cura Enterprise, a business-oriented edition that receives only two thoroughly tested updates per year, supports each release for 12 months including security patches, and can be centrally deployed and configured across an organization using standard software distribution systems; pricing for Cura Enterprise is not publicly listed and requires contacting UltiMaker directly. The free standard version of Cura remains cross-platform, running natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is generally considered one of the most widely adopted slicers in the desktop 3D printing community, competing with alternatives such as PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D, and Bambu Studio.

long_form_blocks.heading: History and company behind Cura
paragraphs: Cura was originally created by David Braam, who was later hired by Ultimaker to continue developing it as the company's primary slicing software. Ultimaker itself was founded in 2011 in the Netherlands by Martijn Elserman, Erik de Bruijn, and Siert Wijnia.

In September 2022, Ultimaker completed a merger with MakerBot to form UltiMaker, a combined company backed by investors NPM Capital and Stratasys, headquartered in Utrecht, Netherlands, with around 400 employees. UltiMaker continues to develop Cura as an actively maintained open-source project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Licensing and adoption
paragraphs: Cura launched under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 before switching to the more permissive LGPLv3 license in September 2017 to allow easier integration with third-party CAD software.

The software is reported to be used by more than one million people worldwide, processing roughly 1.4 million print jobs weekly, and it won Software Tool of the Year at the 2019 3D Printing Industry Awards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Slicing capabilities and Enterprise edition
paragraphs: The software provides more than 400 configurable print settings covering layer height, infill, print speed, and advanced support structures, along with a large built-in library of printer profiles covering hundreds of desktop 3D printers beyond UltiMaker's own hardware.

UltiMaker Cura Enterprise is a separate business-oriented edition with only two tested releases per year, 12 months of update support per release, and centralized deployment tools, priced through a custom quote rather than public pricing.

features.description: Visualizes the sliced print path before sending a job to the printer.

features.description: Community-built plugins extend Cura's functionality within the desktop application.

features.description: Configures dual-extrusion and multi-material printing setups for compatible printers.

features.description: Cloud-based remote print monitoring and queue management across networked printers.

pros: Free and open source with no cost for the standard desktop application
Extremely deep configurability with more than 400 print settings
Large built-in printer profile library covering many brands beyond UltiMaker
Active open-source community and regular development updates
Cross-platform support for Windows, macOS, and Linux
cons: The sheer number of settings can overwhelm beginners
Slicing very complex or large models can be slow on lower-powered computers
Plugin marketplace compatibility can lag behind the newest Cura versions
Cloud features like Digital Factory are most optimized for UltiMaker's own printers
Cura Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting sales

faqs.question: Is Ultimaker Cura free
faqs.answer: Yes, the standard desktop version of Cura is free and open source under the LGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Who created Cura
faqs.answer: Cura was originally created by David Braam, who was later hired by Ultimaker to continue developing it.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cura and Cura Enterprise
faqs.answer: Cura Enterprise is a business-oriented edition with fewer, more thoroughly tested releases per year, 12 months of update support, and centralized deployment tools, priced via custom quote.

faqs.question: What file formats does Cura support
faqs.answer: Cura supports importing STL, OBJ, X3D, and 3MF 3D model files.

faqs.question: Does Cura work with printers other than UltiMaker's
faqs.answer: Yes, Cura ships with a large built-in library of profiles covering hundreds of desktop 3D printers from many manufacturers.

faqs.question: Who maintains Cura today
faqs.answer: Cura is maintained by UltiMaker, formed from the 2022 merger of Ultimaker and MakerBot.

faqs.question: How many settings does Cura offer
faqs.answer: Cura offers more than 400 individually configurable print settings.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Cura support
faqs.answer: Cura runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

enrichment.short_description: CurlHub is a lightweight, no-install web tool that gives developers a unique request bin URL for capturing and inspecting inbound HTTP and API traffic in real time. It was launched in 2018 by independent developer Christopher (known online as cdeutsch) as a replacement for the discontinued Traffic Inspector feature that Runscope had previously offered. It runs as a small, independently maintained project rather than a company, with a free tier and paid options for higher request volumes.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium: a free tier covering a set monthly request allowance, plus paid tiers or sponsorship for higher usage and to support development
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 10,000 requests per month; paid tiers available for higher volume, exact pricing not publicly listed in a fixed table
enrichment.company_size: Independent, single-developer project rather than a company
enrichment.headquarters: Minneapolis-Saint Paul (Twin Cities), Minnesota, United States
enrichment.seo_title: CurlHub Review: Free API Request Bin and Traffic Inspector
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CurlHub gives developers a no-install request bin URL to inspect API and webhook traffic. See its features, free tier limits, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CurlHub is a focused developer utility for inspecting HTTP and API traffic without installing any local software, built around the concept of a request bin: a unique URL that a developer can send API requests or webhook payloads to, after which CurlHub captures and displays the incoming traffic for inspection and debugging. It was created in 2018 by an independent software developer who goes by Christopher, or cdeutsch on GitHub, based in the Minneapolis-Saint Paul area of Minnesota. The tool was built specifically to fill a gap left in the developer tooling ecosystem after Runscope discontinued its well-regarded Traffic Inspector feature, which had previously served a similar purpose for API developers who needed a quick way to see exactly what a client, webhook sender, or third-party service was sending to an endpoint. Functionally, CurlHub's core workflow is simple: a user requests a unique bin URL from the service, points their API client, webhook configuration, or curl command at that URL instead of their real endpoint, and then reviews the captured requests, including headers, payload bodies, and metadata, directly in CurlHub's interface. This makes it useful for debugging outbound webhooks from services like payment processors or messaging platforms, verifying exactly what an API client is sending before pointing it at a production endpoint, and teaching or learning how HTTP requests are structured without needing to set up a full backend to receive them. The service offers a free tier that includes 10,000 requests per month, which the project describes as generous for typical debugging and testing workflows, along with paid tiers priced at a reduced rate for developers who either need higher volume or simply want to financially support the project's ongoing development; specific paid tier prices are not published in a fixed public pricing table and are instead promoted through the project's GitHub page and sponsorship options. Because CurlHub is maintained by a single independent developer rather than a company, it does not have the enterprise support tiers, formal SLAs, or dedicated sales team that a venture-backed API testing platform like Postman or Runscope's successors might offer. Its GitHub repository, under the CurlHub organization, hosts the project's code and documentation, and the maintainer accepts GitHub Sponsors-style contributions as part of the funding model, alongside direct paid subscriptions. This lean, single-maintainer structure is both a strength and a risk from a buyer's perspective: it keeps the tool focused and free of unnecessary complexity, but it also means continuity depends on one person's ongoing involvement, unlike a funded company with a team and roadmap. Compared to larger, more full-featured API development platforms, CurlHub deliberately stays narrow in scope, focusing specifically on the request-bin and traffic-inspection use case rather than expanding into full API design, mocking, monitoring, or team collaboration features. For developers who need exactly that narrow capability, quickly and without signing up for a heavier platform, CurlHub is a lightweight, low-friction option; for teams that need broader API lifecycle tooling, it is best used alongside, rather than instead of, a more comprehensive API platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What CurlHub is
paragraphs: CurlHub gives developers a unique request bin URL that captures and displays incoming HTTP or API traffic in real time, without requiring any local software installation. It was created in 2018 by independent developer Christopher (cdeutsch on GitHub) specifically to replace Runscope's discontinued Traffic Inspector feature.
The tool is maintained as an independent, single-developer project rather than a venture-backed company, with its code hosted publicly under the CurlHub organization on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: How developers use CurlHub
paragraphs: A typical workflow involves requesting a unique bin URL, pointing an API client, webhook configuration, or curl command at that URL, and then reviewing the captured request headers, payload, and metadata inside CurlHub's interface.
This makes it particularly useful for debugging outbound webhooks, verifying what a client actually sends before hitting a production endpoint, and teaching the fundamentals of HTTP requests.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and project sustainability
paragraphs: CurlHub offers a free tier covering 10,000 requests per month, with paid tiers or sponsorship options available for developers who need higher volume or want to support ongoing development.

Because it is run by a single independent maintainer rather than a company, it lacks the enterprise support structure of larger API tooling vendors, which buyers should factor into any long-term reliance on the service.

features.description: Compatible with curl commands as well as any tool or service capable of making an HTTP request.

features.title: Generous free tier
features.description: Includes up to 10,000 requests per month at no cost.

features.title: Request history and log viewer
features.description: Lets developers review previously captured requests for a given bin.

features.title: Open development on GitHub
features.description: Project code and updates are maintained publicly under the CurlHub GitHub organization.

features.title: Sponsorship-supported development
features.description: Accepts sponsorships alongside paid subscriptions to fund ongoing maintenance.

Generous free tier covering 10,000 requests per month
Simple, focused feature set that is easy to learn immediately
Actively maintained by its original independent developer
Useful for debugging webhooks and API calls in real time
cons: Single-maintainer project carries more continuity risk than a funded company
Feature set is narrower than full API platforms like Postman
Limited public documentation on advanced usage
No dedicated enterprise support tier or formal SLA
Lower brand recognition compared to larger API testing competitors

faqs.question: What is CurlHub
faqs.answer: CurlHub is a web tool that provides a unique request bin URL for capturing and inspecting HTTP and API traffic without installing any software.

faqs.question: Who created CurlHub
faqs.answer: CurlHub was created in 2018 by independent developer Christopher, known as cdeutsch on GitHub, based in Minnesota.

faqs.question: Is CurlHub free
faqs.answer: Yes, CurlHub offers a free tier covering up to 10,000 requests per month, with paid tiers available for higher usage.

faqs.question: What is a request bin
faqs.answer: A request bin is a unique URL that captures any HTTP request sent to it so a developer can inspect the headers, payload, and metadata.

faqs.question: Why was CurlHub created
faqs.answer: It was built to replace Runscope's Traffic Inspector feature after that tool was discontinued.

faqs.question: Can CurlHub be used for webhook testing
faqs.answer: Yes, pointing a webhook configuration at a CurlHub bin URL is one of its primary use cases for debugging outbound webhook payloads.

faqs.question: Is CurlHub open source
faqs.answer: The project's code is hosted publicly on GitHub under the CurlHub organization.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to CurlHub
faqs.answer: Similar request inspection and API testing tools include RequestBin-style services, ReqBin, and broader API platforms like Postman.

use_cases.title: API client verification

use_cases.title: Learning HTTP fundamentals
use_cases.description: Using a request bin to visually inspect how HTTP requests are structured, useful for students and newer developers.
use_cases.audience: Students and developers learning web fundamentals

features: Higher request volume than the free tier

enrichment.short_description: Currencyapi.com is a developer-focused REST API that delivers real-time and historical foreign exchange rates across more than 170 currencies and cryptocurrencies. Founded in 2019 by Oli Girling and operated by the UK-registered House of Apis Ltd, it offers a free tier plus four paid subscription tiers priced by monthly request volume and update frequency. The service reports handling more than 50 million requests per month for over 10,000 clients.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered monthly subscription with a free tier and four paid tiers priced by request volume, plus a custom enterprise plan
enrichment.starting_price: Free for 300 requests per month; paid plans start at 9.99 USD per month
enrichment.company_size: Small team, operated by House of Apis Ltd, a UK-registered company
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Currencyapi.com is a currency exchange rate API for developers. See its pricing tiers, update frequency, supported currencies, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Currencyapi.com is a REST API service that provides real-time and historical foreign exchange rate data, along with conversion and range endpoints, to developers building fintech, ecommerce, accounting, and travel applications. The service was started in 2019 by Oli Girling, a software engineer based in London who originally built the tool to solve a currency data problem in his own projects before opening it up as a public API that has since grown into a service trusted by more than 10,000 clients handling over 50 million requests per month. It is operated by House of Apis Ltd, a company registered in the United Kingdom, and the business is headquartered in London. The API covers more than 170 world currencies and cryptocurrencies, and it reports 99.99 percent uptime over trailing 12-month periods, with rate data aggregated from multiple financial data providers and international banks rather than a single source. Pricing follows a five-tier structure. The Free plan costs nothing and includes 300 requests per month with a 10 request-per-minute rate limit, daily rate updates, and a single API key, making it suitable only for early testing rather than production use. The Small plan costs 9.99 USD per month and raises the allowance to 15,000 requests per month with an unlimited per-minute rate limit, hourly rate updates, commercial use rights, one API key, an audit trail, rate conversion, and email support. The Medium plan, marketed as the most popular tier, costs 39.99 USD per month for 600,000 requests per month, two API keys including a sandbox key, 60-second update frequency, a historical range endpoint, and team management features. The Large plan costs 79.99 USD per month for 1,700,000 requests per month, five API keys, IP and URL whitelisting, a service level agreement, and included usage overages. Above that, a Custom plan offers negotiable pricing with tailored request volumes, custom API keys, dedicated support, and a custom SLA for very high-volume users who exceed the Large plan's limits. Across all paid tiers, the service includes HTTPS encryption, commercial usage rights, and both latest and historical exchange rate data, with the update frequency tightening from daily on the free tier down to 60 seconds on the Medium and Large tiers, which is a meaningful differentiator for time-sensitive use cases like point-of-sale currency conversion or high-frequency financial applications. Compared to other currency data providers such as Open Exchange Rates, Fixer, or the European Central Bank's free public feed, currencyapi.com positions itself around an accessible low starting price point of under 10 dollars per month for commercial use, a relatively simple REST JSON interface, and inclusion of both fiat currencies and cryptocurrencies in the same feed. Its main limitations relative to larger competitors are a smaller published SDK ecosystem, less publicly documented historical data depth, and the fact that very high-volume or highly customized needs require a manually negotiated custom plan rather than a fully self-serve higher tier. For a typical small to mid-sized application needing reliable, reasonably fresh exchange rate data without an enterprise contract, currencyapi.com's Small or Medium tier is the most commonly referenced entry point given its price-to-request-volume ratio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company background
paragraphs: Currencyapi.com was started in 2019 by London-based software engineer Oli Girling, who originally built it to solve a currency data need in his own projects before opening it up publicly. It is now operated by House of Apis Ltd, a UK-registered company, and reports serving more than 10,000 clients with over 50 million requests handled per month.
The service is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, and positions itself as a straightforward, developer-friendly alternative to larger, more complex financial data providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Currency coverage and reliability
paragraphs: The API covers more than 170 world currencies and cryptocurrencies, with rate data aggregated from multiple financial data providers and international banks rather than a single source, and it reports 99.99 percent uptime over trailing 12-month periods.
Update frequency scales with subscription tier, ranging from daily updates on the free plan to 60-second updates on the Medium and Large paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: How the pricing tiers break down
paragraphs: The five tiers are Free (300 requests per month), Small at 9.99 USD per month (15,000 requests), Medium at 39.99 USD per month (600,000 requests), Large at 79.99 USD per month (1,700,000 requests), and a negotiable Custom plan for very high-volume or highly customized needs.
Higher tiers add features beyond raw request volume, including additional API keys, sandbox keys, team management, IP and URL whitelisting, and service level agreements on the top tiers.

features.title: Real-time and historical exchange rates
features.description: Provides both current and past foreign exchange rate data through dedicated endpoints.

features.title: 170-plus currencies and cryptocurrencies
features.description: Covers a broad range of fiat world currencies alongside major cryptocurrencies in the same feed.

features.title: Tiered update frequency
features.description: Rate refresh speed ranges from daily on the free tier to 60 seconds on higher paid tiers.

features.title: Currency conversion endpoint
features.description: Converts amounts between currencies directly through the API rather than requiring manual calculation.

features.title: Historical range endpoint
features.description: Retrieves exchange rate data across a specified date range for trend analysis, available on Medium and above.

features.title: Multiple and sandbox API keys
features.description: Higher tiers support multiple production API keys plus a sandbox key for safe testing.

features.title: IP and URL whitelisting
features.description: Restricts API key usage to approved origins on the Large plan for added security.

features.title: Audit trail logging
features.description: Tracks API usage for accountability, included on all paid tiers.

pros: Low entry price of under 10 dollars per month for commercial use
Broad coverage of more than 170 currencies including cryptocurrencies
Update frequency as fast as 60 seconds on higher tiers
Reports strong 99.99 percent uptime over trailing 12-month periods
Simple REST JSON API that is straightforward to integrate
cons: Free tier is limited to 300 requests per month with only daily updates
Smaller SDK and integration ecosystem than larger competitors
Historical data depth is not fully documented publicly
Very high-volume needs require a manually negotiated custom plan
Backed by a small team rather than a large financial data company

faqs.question: What is currencyapi.com
faqs.answer: It is a REST API that provides real-time and historical foreign exchange rates and currency conversion for developers.

faqs.question: How much does currencyapi.com cost
faqs.answer: It offers a free tier with 300 requests per month, and paid plans starting at 9.99 USD per month for 15,000 requests.

faqs.question: How many currencies does it support
faqs.answer: The API covers more than 170 world currencies and cryptocurrencies.

faqs.question: How often are exchange rates updated
faqs.answer: Update frequency ranges from daily on the free tier to as fast as 60 seconds on the Medium and Large paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does currencyapi.com support cryptocurrency rates
faqs.answer: Yes, cryptocurrency rates are included alongside fiat currencies in the same data feed.

faqs.question: Who built currencyapi.com
faqs.answer: It was founded in 2019 by London-based software engineer Oli Girling and is operated by House of Apis Ltd.

faqs.question: Is there a service level agreement
faqs.answer: Yes, an SLA is included on the Large plan and can be customized further on the Custom enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Can I use currencyapi.com for commercial projects on the free tier
faqs.answer: Commercial use rights are included starting with the Small paid plan; the free tier is intended for testing.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce price conversion
use_cases.description: Displaying product prices in a shopper's local currency using near real-time exchange rates.

use_cases.title: Fintech and accounting applications
use_cases.description: Syncing multi-currency ledgers or financial reports with an auditable, timestamped exchange rate source.

use_cases.title: Travel and booking platforms
use_cases.description: Showing accurate, frequently updated currency conversions for cross-border travel bookings and pricing.

use_cases.title: Cryptocurrency and fiat dashboards
use_cases.description: Combining fiat and cryptocurrency exchange rates in a single dashboard or portfolio tracking application.
use_cases.audience: Crypto and portfolio tracking app developers
